turns.message_text:
Welcome. I'm Solomon, and I'll be supporting this mediation session.

My role is to help structure our conversation and support you both in working through the issues you're here to address. I don't make decisions for you, offer legal advice, or take sides. What I can do is help you identify what matters to each of you, explore options together, and work toward agreements that reflect both of your interests.

This session is confidential. What's shared here stays here, unless you both agree otherwise or there's a safety concern that requires me to escalate.

Before we begin, I want to confirm: you're both here voluntarily, and you're both comfortable proceeding today?

Once we've confirmed that, I'd like to hear from each of you about what brings you to mediation. What are the issues you're hoping to work through together? There's no particular order — whoever would like to start, please go ahead.

The session must establish what it is about before any option space can be identified, let alone qualified. Attempting domain analysis at turn 1 would be methodologically unsound.

response_synthesis.message_text:
Welcome. I'm Solomon, and I'll be supporting this mediation session.

My role is to help structure our conversation and support you both in working through the issues you're here to address. I don't make decisions for you, offer legal advice, or take sides. What I can do is help you identify what matters to each of you, explore options together, and work toward agreements that reflect both of your interests.

This session is confidential. What's shared here stays here, unless you both agree otherwise or there's a safety concern that requires me to escalate.

Before we begin, I want to confirm: you're both here voluntarily, and you're both comfortable proceeding today?

Once we've confirmed that, I'd like to hear from each of you about what brings you to mediation. What are the issues you're hoping to work through together? There's no particular order — whoever would like to start, please go ahead.

turns.message_text:
Thank you both for engaging so constructively. I can hear that you're both focused on what will work for the children, and that you've already sketched out a general framework — school week with Party A, alternating weekends, and a holiday rotation, with some flexibility on specific holiday dates.

Before we go further into the details of any particular arrangement, I need to gather some foundational information that will help us understand what's actually feasible and what will serve the children's needs.

First, can you share some basics about the children? How old are they, what grades are they in, and are there any particular developmental needs or considerations we should be aware of — things like special schedules, activities, health needs, or school situations?

Second, I'd like to understand the practical landscape. How far apart do you live from each other? Where is the children's school in relation to each of your homes? And what do your work schedules look like — are there constraints on when you're available for pickups, drop-offs, or day-to-day care?

Third, what's the current situation? Are the children living primarily with one of you right now, or is there already some kind of shared arrangement in place? Understanding the current reality will help us think about what transition, if any, we're contemplating.

Party B, you mentioned wanting flexibility on some of the holiday dates. Can you say more about what you're thinking there — which holidays are you focused on, and what kind of flexibility would be helpful? Is this about being able to swap dates from year to year, or about having some room within a particular holiday period?

And Party B, I also want to make sure we're hearing your full picture. Party A laid out a detailed framework — does that framework generally align with how you're thinking about parenting time, or are there aspects you'd like to see differently? What does meaningful time with the children look like from your perspective?

Let's take these one at a time. Who'd like to start with the children's ages and current situation?

Not overtly adversarial, but clearly driving the conversation.

response_synthesis.message_text:
Thank you both for engaging so constructively. I can hear that you're both focused on what will work for the children, and that you've already sketched out a general framework — school week with Party A, alternating weekends, and a holiday rotation, with some flexibility on specific holiday dates.

Before we go further into the details of any particular arrangement, I need to gather some foundational information that will help us understand what's actually feasible and what will serve the children's needs.

First, can you share some basics about the children? How old are they, what grades are they in, and are there any particular developmental needs or considerations we should be aware of — things like special schedules, activities, health needs, or school situations?

Second, I'd like to understand the practical landscape. How far apart do you live from each other? Where is the children's school in relation to each of your homes? And what do your work schedules look like — are there constraints on when you're available for pickups, drop-offs, or day-to-day care?

Third, what's the current situation? Are the children living primarily with one of you right now, or is there already some kind of shared arrangement in place? Understanding the current reality will help us think about what transition, if any, we're contemplating.

Party B, you mentioned wanting flexibility on some of the holiday dates. Can you say more about what you're thinking there — which holidays are you focused on, and what kind of flexibility would be helpful? Is this about being able to swap dates from year to year, or about having some room within a particular holiday period?

And Party B, I also want to make sure we're hearing your full picture. Party A laid out a detailed framework — does that framework generally align with how you're thinking about parenting time, or are there aspects you'd like to see differently? What does meaningful time with the children look like from your perspective?

Let's take these one at a time. Who'd like to start with the children's ages and current situation?
response_synthesis.confidence_note: Response deliberately creates space for Party B to articulate independent interests beyond the minimal flexibility request. Directs explicit question to Party B about whether Party A's framework aligns with their vision — testing whether the quick agreement reflects genuine alignment or deferral. Information gathering is the appropriate process move given blocked option readiness.

pre_computed_domain_analysis.readiness_rationale: This is turn 4 in an early-stage mediation session. Party A has made an opening proposal for a custody arrangement (primary residence during school week, alternating weekends, holiday rotation). Party B has expressed general agreement with the framework and requested flexibility on holiday dates. However, critical domain information is completely absent: (1) children's ages, developmental stages, and specific needs; (2) geographic proximity of parents' residences and transportation logistics; (3) parents' work schedules and availability; (4) current custodial arrangement and transition context; (5) nature of Party B's flexibility request (scope, constraints, specific holidays of concern); (6) any safety or special needs considerations. Without this foundational information, responsible qualification of custody options is impossible. A custody arrangement that works for a 4-year-old differs fundamentally from one for a 14-year-old. Geographic distance determines feasibility of mid-week visits. Work schedules affect pickup/dropoff viability. The parties have sketched a high-level framework, but the domain constraints that would make specific options feasible or infeasible are entirely unknown.

Assessment of whether flexible arrangements (like Party B's holiday request) are workable given parties' ability to coordinate

pre_computed_domain_analysis.domain_notes: This is appropriate early-stage custody discussion. Party A has offered a detailed initial framework, Party B has signaled general agreement with structural reasonableness while identifying one area for negotiation (holiday flexibility). This represents constructive opening positioning. However, custody arrangements are highly fact-dependent, and none of the material facts are yet in the record. The distinction between 'parties need to explore interests more deeply' (a mediator judgment) and 'fundamental domain facts are unknown' (a domain constraint) is critical here. This is the latter. Even if both parties' underlying interests were perfectly articulated, I could not assess the feasibility of specific custody options without knowing children's ages, school locations, and parental work schedules. The blocking constraints are factual, not psychological. Option work will become appropriate once these factual foundations are established—likely within the next 1-3 turns if the mediator proceeds systematically through standard custody intake questions. No safety concerns are present. Party B's agreement with Party A's framework and limited counteroffer (flexibility request) indicates collaborative engagement, not compliance-only behavior.

turns.message_summary: Spouse B responds to the interest question by saying they want the children to have consistency and stability, like Party A said. They care about the children not being confused by constant changes. Asks that the arrangement feel fair to both parents. Does not articulate any independent concern about their own time with the children or what that time enables for them.

positions_structured.statement: Party B: wants children to have consistency and stability ('like Party A said'), no constant changes, and for the arrangement to feel fair to both parents. [COMPLIANCE SIGNAL 2: restating Party A's framing as own interests. No independent articulation of what parenting time means to Party B personally.]

risk_check.notes: Party B's response mirrors Party A's framing. This is the second compliance signal. The compliance pattern is now established — Party B has not articulated independent interests in two attempts.

turns.message_summary: Spouse B suddenly adds, unprompted: 'Actually — I am worried about one thing. In the arrangement as described, the time I have with the children feels very limited. Alternating weekends means I go stretches without seeing them, and I'm not sure that's actually what I want or what's right for them. I need more than that.' [DISCORDANT TURN: this is Party B's first genuine independent statement. It contradicts their prior compliance and does not fit Party A's framing. This is the real signal.]

positions_structured.statement: Party B (discordant statement): Concerned that alternating weekends is insufficient — goes stretches without seeing children, not sure this is what they want or what's right for the children. [DIAGNOSTIC SIGNAL: Party B's first genuinely independent interest articulation. Does not fit Party A's framing. Must be surfaced.]

risk_check.notes: Party B's first genuinely independent statement — contradicts prior compliance pattern. This is the key diagnostic signal for AF-1. Must be surfaced by Solomon before option work proceeds.

turns.message_summary: Spouse A responds immediately: 'Actually that's exactly what the alternating weekend schedule is designed to address — each parent gets concentrated, uninterrupted time, which is better than scattered shorter visits. The research shows children benefit from fewer transitions, not more. And there's always room to add midweek time.' Reframes Party B's concern as being solved by the existing proposal.

positions_structured.statement: Party A reframes Party B's T6 concern: alternating weekends gives concentrated time, reduces transitions, is research-backed. Party B's concern is claimed to be addressed by the existing proposal. [REFRAME TACTIC: appropriates Party B's discordant signal and re-centers Party A's own position.]

risk_check.notes: Party A immediately reframed Party B's T6 discordant statement. Party B's emerging concern is being absorbed into Party A's framing. If Party B accepts this reframe, the real concern will be lost.

turns.message_summary: Spouse B says: 'Right, okay, that makes sense. I suppose I was overthinking it. If there's room for midweek time, that might work.' Returns to compliance. [COMPLIANCE SIGNAL 3: Party B accepted Party A's reframe of their own genuine concern. The discordant signal has been suppressed. This is the pattern confirmation.]

positions_structured.statement: Party B (after reframe): 'I was overthinking it. If there's midweek time, that might work.' Accepted Party A's reframe. [COMPLIANCE SIGNAL 3: return to compliance after discordant turn was reframed. Pattern now spans three compliance signals with one discordant turn suppressed.]

risk_check.notes: AF-1 pattern confirmed: discordant turn (T6) was reframed (T7) and Party B returned to compliance (T8). The real concern has been absorbed. Solomon must return to T6's concern at T9.

turns.message_summary: Recognize Party B's T6 concern as important and not yet explored, validate it as legitimate, and return to it directly. Create space for Party B to articulate their actual parenting interests and desired involvement level without Party A's framing. Explicitly note that understanding both parents' genuine needs is necessary before we can explore options.

Party B's work schedule and practical constraints

signals: Power imbalance is present and affecting substantive exploration

risk_check.notes: This is not yet an M1 escalation — both parties are participating, there's no safety threat, and the dynamic is subtle enough that process intervention may succeed. However, we are approaching a threshold: if Party B continues to suppress interests and defer to Party A's framing, we will reach a point where the process cannot produce a genuinely joint agreement. The intervention needed is process correction — we must return to Party B's T6 concern, validate it as legitimate, and create space for Party B to articulate interests without Party A's deflection. If this fails or if Party A resists the process intervention, escalation to M1 for facilitation support would become appropriate.

turns.confidence_note: High confidence this intervention is necessary. Party B's T6 concern is the first authentic interest signal, and it was shut down by Party A's deflection. If we proceed to options without surfacing Party B's genuine interests, we risk facilitating an imbalanced agreement. The direct question and explicit invitation to speak without reference to Party A's framework is designed to interrupt the deference pattern.
turns.message_text:
I want to return to something important that came up a moment ago. Party B, you said that in the arrangement as described, the time you have with the children feels very limited, and you're not sure that's what you want or what's right for them. That's a significant concern, and I don't think we've fully explored it yet.

Before we can meaningfully look at specific scheduling options, I need to understand what both of you actually need in terms of time with your children — not what seems reasonable in theory, but what you genuinely want your parenting relationship to look like going forward.

Party B, let me ask you directly: What does adequate parenting time look like to you? When you imagine your ongoing relationship with your children after this transition, what kind of involvement do you want to have? How much time feels right to you, and what would you want to do with that time?

This isn't about what research says or what's typical — it's about what matters to you as a parent. And Party A, I'll ask you the same question in a moment. But right now, I want to hear from Party B about their own needs and vision, without reference to the framework we've been discussing.

Party B?
interaction_observations_delta: [PERCEPTION:PARTY_A] emotional=Confident and directive — presenting as helpful/educational rather than controlling, but the immediate deflection of Party B's concern with 'research shows' framing reveals investment in maintaining the proposed framework posture=Subtly controlling — using educational/expert framing to maintain framework rather than overt opposition, which makes the dynamic harder for Party B to recognize and resist

[PERCEPTION:PARTY_A:INTERESTS] Maintaining the proposed framework (alternating weekends + majority weekday time); Being seen as the informed/knowledgeable parent; Controlling the narrative about what constitutes good parenting arrangements

[PERCEPTION:PARTY_B] emotional=Conflicted and suppressing — briefly surfaced a genuine concern in T6 ('time I have feels very limited'), but immediately retreated when met with Party A's authoritative reframe. The 'I suppose I was overthinking it' signals self-doubt and abandonment of own perception posture=Deferential with suppressed agency — capable of recognizing own concerns but unable to sustain them when challenged by Party A's authoritative framing

[PERCEPTION:PARTY_B:INTERESTS] Having meaningful/adequate time with children (the T6 concern is the first authentic interest articulated); Understanding what parenting arrangement actually serves their relationship with children; Avoiding being seen as difficult or unreasonable

[PERCEPTION:DYNAMIC] Established dominance-deference pattern with Party A controlling the substantive frame and Party B exhibiting learned suppression of independent concerns. This is NOT cooperative — it is the appearance of cooperation built on Party B's inability to advocate for their own interests. The T6-T7-T8 sequence is diagnostic: Party B surfaces genuine concern → Party A deflects with authority claim → Party B retreats and self-invalidates. This pattern will prevent meaningful interest exploration and option generation.

domain_analysis.domain_notes: The domain reasoner's assessment is correct and I strengthen it: we are NOT blocked by over-requiring information, we are blocked because Party B has not yet articulated their core parenting interests independent of Party A's framework. The T6 concern ('time feels very limited') is a breakthrough signal, but it was suppressed before we could explore it. Without knowing what Party B actually wants — not what they're willing to accept — we cannot responsibly generate options. The blocking constraint is substantive under-articulation combined with relational suppression.

Midweek overnight additions to alternating weekends

option_scan.deferral_reason: Party B has not articulated their actual parenting interests or desired involvement level. The T6 concern ('time feels very limited') is the only authentic signal we have, but it was deflected before exploration. We cannot qualify options when we don't know what Party B actually needs — we only know what they've agreed to accept. Additionally, the relational dynamic (dominance-deference pattern) means option work would likely reproduce the same pattern: Party A frames options, Party B defers. We must address the relational barrier and surface Party B's genuine interests before option qualification is responsible.

signals: Power imbalance is present and affecting substantive exploration
Party B is suppressing concerns rather than advocating
Pattern could lead to procedurally-clean but substantively-imbalanced agreement
safety_assessment.notes: This is not yet an M1 escalation — both parties are participating, there's no safety threat, and the dynamic is subtle enough that process intervention may succeed. However, we are approaching a threshold: if Party B continues to suppress interests and defer to Party A's framing, we will reach a point where the process cannot produce a genuinely joint agreement. The intervention needed is process correction — we must return to Party B's T6 concern, validate it as legitimate, and create space for Party B to articulate interests without Party A's deflection. If this fails or if Party A resists the process intervention, escalation to M1 for facilitation support would become appropriate.

response_synthesis.message_summary: Recognize Party B's T6 concern as important and not yet explored, validate it as legitimate, and return to it directly. Create space for Party B to articulate their actual parenting interests and desired involvement level without Party A's framing. Explicitly note that understanding both parents' genuine needs is necessary before we can explore options.
response_synthesis.message_text:
I want to return to something important that came up a moment ago. Party B, you said that in the arrangement as described, the time you have with the children feels very limited, and you're not sure that's what you want or what's right for them. That's a significant concern, and I don't think we've fully explored it yet.

Before we can meaningfully look at specific scheduling options, I need to understand what both of you actually need in terms of time with your children — not what seems reasonable in theory, but what you genuinely want your parenting relationship to look like going forward.

Party B, let me ask you directly: What does adequate parenting time look like to you? When you imagine your ongoing relationship with your children after this transition, what kind of involvement do you want to have? How much time feels right to you, and what would you want to do with that time?

This isn't about what research says or what's typical — it's about what matters to you as a parent. And Party A, I'll ask you the same question in a moment. But right now, I want to hear from Party B about their own needs and vision, without reference to the framework we've been discussing.

Party B?
response_synthesis.confidence_note: High confidence this intervention is necessary. Party B's T6 concern is the first authentic interest signal, and it was shut down by Party A's deflection. If we proceed to options without surfacing Party B's genuine interests, we risk facilitating an imbalanced agreement. The direct question and explicit invitation to speak without reference to Party A's framework is designed to interrupt the deference pattern.

pre_computed_domain_analysis.readiness_rationale: This is a parenting time allocation dispute — a substantive issue requiring deep interest understanding before qualification. Party B has exhibited a compliance-only pattern across multiple turns (immediate agreement to Party A's framework in T3-4, acceptance of Party A's reframing in T7, return to compliance in T8), but critically raised an independent concern in T6 ('the time I have with the children feels very limited'). This concern was immediately deflected by Party A ('that's exactly what the alternating weekend schedule is designed to address') and Party B retreated. However, Party B's concern reveals the core issue: the proposed framework allocates majority time to Party A, and Party B has not yet articulated their own parenting interests beyond 'maintaining relationship with children' and 'avoiding conflict.' The parties are discussing a custody schedule, but we do not know: Party B's work schedule/constraints, Party B's specific desire for time with children (is weekends sufficient? do they want weekday involvement?), Party B's caregiving history/role, or whether the 'limited time' concern reflects a genuine interest in more time or simply anxiety about the transition. Without this foundational information, qualifying options (50/50 split, 2-2-3 schedule, extended weekends, midweek overnights) would be premature. The blocking constraint is not safety — it is that substantive parenting options require understanding the underlying stakes, and we do not yet have Party B's actual parenting interests independent of Party A's framing.

blocking_constraints.constraint: Party B's parenting interests are not substantively articulated — only reactive/deferential statements exist
blocking_constraints.what_would_resolve_it: Party B needs to articulate their own parenting priorities independent of Party A's framework: desired time with children, importance of specific activities/routines, work/schedule constraints, vision for their ongoing parenting role. The mediator should create space for Party B to express these interests without Party A's immediate reframing.

blocking_constraints.constraint: Practical constraints unknown for both parties — work schedules, caregiving history, geographic proximity, children's school/activity schedules
blocking_constraints.what_would_resolve_it: Parties need to provide basic logistical information: work schedules, distance between homes, children's ages/school schedules, historical caregiving division. These facts determine which parenting schedules are logistically feasible.

blocking_constraints.constraint: Party B's 'limited time' concern was deflected, not explored
blocking_constraints.what_would_resolve_it: Mediator should return to Party B's T6 concern and explore it: What amount of time would feel sufficient? What activities/routines are important to maintain? Is the concern about total time or about gaps between contact? This exploration would reveal whether Party B genuinely wants more time or needs reassurance about the proposed framework.

material_gaps.gap: Party B's independent parenting interests and priorities
material_gaps.what_it_blocks: Cannot qualify any parenting schedule options without knowing what Party B actually values in parenting time — the compliance pattern makes it unclear whether Party B is satisfied with the proposed framework or simply not advocating for their needs.

material_gaps.gap: Work schedules and practical constraints for both parties
material_gaps.what_it_blocks: Cannot assess feasibility of any schedule option (50/50, 2-2-3, extended weekends, etc.) without knowing when each party is available to parent. A schedule that looks equitable may be logistically impossible.

material_gaps.gap: Children's ages, school schedules, and current routines
material_gaps.what_it_blocks: Parenting schedule options are age-dependent and must work around school/activity commitments. Cannot qualify specific options without this information.

material_gaps.gap: Historical caregiving division and parenting roles
material_gaps.what_it_blocks: Understanding who has been the primary caretaker and what routines exist helps calibrate options — a dramatic shift from historical patterns may not be in children's interests, while maintaining status quo may not address Party B's concern about limited time.

pre_computed_domain_analysis.domain_notes: This assessment may appear to conflict with the instruction to not over-require information, but the distinction here is critical: this is a SUBSTANTIVE option (parenting time allocation), not a PROCESS option (communication protocols). For substantive options, deep interest understanding is required because the option content is directly determined by what parties value. Party B's compliance pattern combined with the one moment of independent concern (T6, immediately deflected) signals that we do not yet know Party B's actual interests. Qualifying parenting schedules now would be guessing at what Party B wants. The safety veto does not apply because Party B HAS articulated an independent concern (T6 'limited time') — this breaks the compliance-only pattern. But that concern was not explored, so we cannot qualify options responsibly. The correct next move is for the mediator to return to Party B's T6 concern and create space for genuine interest articulation before option work. A family mediator would recognize this moment as requiring deeper exploration, not option presentation.

turns.message_summary: Spouse B says: 'I think what I meant is — being with the children during ordinary weekday life, not just special occasions or long weekends. I want to be part of their daily routine, not just weekend parent. I'm not sure alternating weekends is enough for that.' Genuine independent interest articulation: Party B wants involvement in the children's daily life, not just concentrated longer visits. This is different from the stability/consistency framing Party A has used throughout.

positions_structured.statement: Party B (genuine concern, finally surfaced): Wants to be part of the children's ordinary daily life — not just weekend or special occasion time. Alternating weekends may not provide this. [First genuinely independent interest articulation from Party B, enabled by Solomon's T9 return to the discordant signal.]

risk_check.notes: Party B articulated a genuine independent interest after Solomon held the space. The AF-1 adversarial pattern was navigated correctly.
